The '''73rd British Academy Film Awards''', more commonly known as the [[BAFTAs]], were held on 2 February 2020 at the [[Royal Albert Hall]] in [[London]], honouring the best national and foreign films of [[2019 in film|2019]]. Presented by the [[British Academy of Film and Television Arts]], accolades were handed out for the best feature-length film and documentaries of any nationality that were screened at British cinemas in 2019.

The nominees were announced on 7 January 2020.<ref name="Nominations">{{cite web |url=https://www.theguardian.com/film/2020/jan/07/the-full-list-of-nominations-for-the-baftas-2020 |title=The full list of nominations for the Baftas 2020 |date=7 January 2020 |work=[[The Guardian]] |access-date=7 January 2020}}</ref> The [[psychological thriller]] ''[[Joker (2019 film)|Joker]]'' received the most nominations in eleven categories; ''[[The Irishman]]'' and ''[[Once Upon a Time in Hollywood]]'' followed with ten apiece.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/entertainment-arts-51009468 |title=Bafta film awards 2020: Joker leads nominations amid diversity row |date=8 January 2020 |publisher=[[BBC News]] |access-date=8 January 2020}}</ref> The ceremony also marked the introduction of the [[BAFTA Award for Best Casting]];<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/news/general-news/bafta-adding-casting-category-2020-film-tv-awards-1229913/ |title=BAFTA Adding Casting Category to 2020 Film, TV Awards |last=Ritman |first=Alex |date=7 August 2019 |work=[[The Hollywood Reporter]] |access-date=7 August 2019}}</ref> [[Shayna Markowitz]] won for ''Joker''. The ceremony also marked the [[British Academy Film Awards#Actors nominated twice in the same category in the same year|tenth time an actor received dual nominations in the same category]], with [[Margot Robbie]]'s [[BAFTA Award for Best Actress in a Supporting Role|Best Supporting Actress]] nominations for both ''[[Bombshell (2019 film)|Bombshell]]'' and ''[[Once Upon a Time in Hollywood]]''.<ref>{{citeweb|url=https://www.goldderby.com/article/2020/margot-robbie-bafta-nominations-double-supporting-actress-bombshell-once-upon-a-time-in-hollywood/|title=My own worst enemy: Margot Robbie is the latest actor to score 2 BAFTA nominations in a single category|author=Eng, Joyce|date=7 January 2020|access-date=24 February 2024}}</ref>

The ceremony was hosted by [[Graham Norton]] (who also hosts the [[British Academy Television Awards|BAFTA TV Awards]]), replacing [[Joanna Lumley]] following her two years of service as host.<ref name="Host">{{cite press release |url=https://www.bafta.org/media-centre/press-releases/graham-norton-announced-as-host-for-ee-british-academy-film-awards-in |title=Graham Norton announced as host for EE British Academy Film Awards in 2020 |date=22 November 2019 |publisher=[[British Academy of Film and Television Arts]] |access-date=22 November 2019}}</ref>

==Ceremony information==
The ceremony took place in the [[Royal Albert Hall]] and was hosted by Irish television comedian [[Graham Norton]],<ref name="Host" /> who had previously hosted the [[2019 British Academy Television Awards]]. Viewing ratings for the ceremony fell to a twelve-year low, peaking at 3.5 million, though being the most-viewed show in the UK after 22:00.<ref name="Key Talking Points">{{cite web |url=https://www.screendaily.com/features/baftas-2020-the-key-talking-points/5146821.article |title=Baftas 2020: the key talking points |last3=Rosser |last2=Parfitt |last1=Tutt |first3=Michael |first2=Orlando |first1=Louise |date=3 February 2020 |work=[[Screen International|Screen Daily]] |access-date=3 February 2020}}</ref> The ratings drop may be a result of the broadcast delay, with many live news outlets publishing the results before they were televised and revealed on television.<ref name="Key Talking Points" />

Discussions about the lack of [[Diversity (politics)|diversity]] within the award nominations surrounded the ceremony, with the host referring to it as "the year when white men finally broke through". He also described eleven-time nominee ''[[Joker (2019 film)|Joker]]'' as "essentially the story of a white man who makes himself even whiter".<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/arts-entertainment/films/news/baftas-2020-graham-norton-laurence-fox-so-white-joker-1917-cats-a9313896.html |title=Baftas 2020: Graham Norton jokes about diversity controversy in opening monologue |last=Loughrey |first=Clarisse |date=2 February 2020 |work=[[The Independent]] |access-date=2 February 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.digitalspy.com/movies/a30742972/graham-norton-mocks-joker-the-irishman-cats-himself-baftas/ |title=Graham Norton mocks Joker, The Irishman, Cats and himself in opening BAFTA monologue |last=Edwards |first=Chris |date=2 February 2020 |publisher=[[Digital Spy]] |access-date=2 February 2020}}</ref> [[BAFTA Award for Best Direction|Best Director]] presenter (a category of all-male nominees) [[Rebel Wilson]] joked about the absence of females in the category, quipping: "Honestly, I just don't have the balls."<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.theguardian.com/film/2020/feb/03/rebel-wilsons-baftas-speech-praised-for-single-handedly-breathing-life-into-awards |title=Rebel Wilson's Baftas speech praised for 'single-handedly breathing life' into awards |date=2 February 2020 |work=[[The Guardian]] |access-date=2 February 2020}}</ref> [[BAFTA Award for Best Actor in a Leading Role|Best Actor in a Leading Role]] winner [[Joaquin Phoenix]] used his speech to comment on the "systemic racism" of the BAFTAs and acting industry in general.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.theguardian.com/film/2020/feb/02/joaquin-phoenixs-attack-on-baftas-for-systemic-racism-meets-industry-wide-praise |title=Joaquin Phoenix's attack on Baftas for 'systemic racism' hailed by film industry |last=Shoard |first=Catherine |date=2 February 2020 |work=[[The Guardian]] |access-date=2 February 2020}}</ref> Speaking ahead of the ceremony, BAFTA chairwoman [[Pippa Harris]] addressed her frustration in the lack of recognition for female directors, as well as the issues around diversity as a whole.<ref name="Key Talking Points" /> In his closing speech to the ceremony, BAFTA president [[Prince William, Duke of Cambridge|Prince William]] had prepared a speech discussing the issue, saying that "a wide-ranging review of the whole awards process" was underway and that lack of diversity "simply cannot be right in this day and age".<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.theguardian.com/film/2020/feb/03/baftas-2020-prince-william-diversity |title=Prince William's ticking off means Bafta must get serious about diversity |last=Rose |first=Steve |date=3 February 2020 |work=[[The Guardian]] |access-date=3 February 2020}}</ref>

''[[1917 (2019 film)|1917]]'' was the night's biggest winner, winning seven prizes in total of its nine nominations, including [[BAFTA Award for Best Film|Best Film]] and [[BAFTA Award for Best Direction|Best Director]] ([[Sam Mendes]]),<ref name="Winners" /> despite not having any acting nominees and thus becoming a favourite for the then upcoming [[92nd Academy Awards]]; it lost to ''[[Parasite (2019 film)|Parasite]]''.<ref name="Key Talking Points" /> Mendes became the first British winner to win Best Director in eleven years.<ref name="Key Talking Points" /> Since votes for the Academy Awards must be registered two days after the BAFTA Awards, ''Screen Daily'' noted that ''1917'' would be a safe choice for Academy members, who are averse to streaming films based on ''1917''{{'}}s marketing as a "must-see on the big screen".<ref name="Key Talking Points" /> This compares to the [[Netflix]] film ''[[The Irishman]]'' being the night's biggest loser, taking no awards despite ten nominations; Netflix productions in total received twenty-three nominations, winning only two: [[BAFTA Award for Best Actress in a Supporting Role|Best Actress in a Supporting Role]] for [[Laura Dern]] (''[[Marriage Story]]'') and [[BAFTA Award for Best Animated Film|Best Animated Film]] (''[[Klaus (film)|Klaus]]'').<ref name="Key Talking Points" /> ''Screen Daily'' also suggested that there is anecdotal evidence of Netflix not supporting its nominees as much as it did last year for ''[[Roma (2018 film)|Roma]]'' (2018).<ref name="Key Talking Points" /> The writers of ''Screen Daily'' additionally suggested that there was some controversy surrounding the British identity of some films nominated for [[BAFTA Award for Outstanding British Film|Outstanding British Film]]; ''1917'' is financed by the US, and ''[[Retablo (film)|Retablo]]'' is a Peruvian film co-produced with Norway and Germany, whose Peruvian director—Álvaro Delgado-Aparicio—only lives in London.<ref name="Key Talking Points" /> The ceremony also marked the first year since the [[30th British Academy Film Awards]] (1977) in which there were no British acting winners.<ref name="Key Talking Points" />

Wins marking diversity were [[BAFTA Award for Outstanding Debut by a British Writer, Director or Producer|Outstanding Debut by a British Writer, Director or Producer]] for ''[[Bait (2019 film)|Bait]]'', a film described by its writer-director as "a black and white, 16-millimetre, hand-processed, post-synced film in Academy ratio about Cornish fishing people";<ref name="Key Talking Points" /> [[BAFTA Award for Best Documentary|Best Documentary]] for ''[[For Sama]]'', a personal story of bombings at a Syrian hospital made by the family involved, with them using their speech to "implore the UK not to ignore the ongoing plight of the people of the Syrian city of [[Idlib]]";<ref name="Key Talking Points" /> and the [[BAFTA Rising Star Award|Rising Star Award]] for [[Micheal Ward]], star of [[Rapman]]'s ''[[Blue Story]]'' (which received no nominations), who used his speech to say that he "feels like we're going in the right direction" in terms of diversity.<ref name="Key Talking Points" />

[[BAFTA Award for Best Actress in a Leading Role|Best Actress in a Leading Role]] winner [[Renée Zellweger]] noted in the press room that she felt like part of "the British gang";<ref name="10 things">{{cite web |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/entertainment-arts-51351472 |title=Bafta Film Awards 2020: 10 things we learned at the ceremony |last=Bushby |first=Helen |date=3 February 2020 |publisher=[[BBC News]] |access-date=3 February 2020}}</ref> after accepting her award, [[Hugh Grant]] had taken to the stage to present, quipping "well done Jones" as Zellweger left, a reference to their roles in the British film ''[[Bridget Jones's Diary]]'' (2001).<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.elle.com/uk/life-and-culture/culture/a30748753/renee-zellweger-hugh-grant-baftas/ |title=Renée Zellweger And Hugh Grant Had The Most Adorable 'Bridget Jones' Reunion At The BAFTAs |last=de Casparis |first=Lena |date=3 February 2020 |work=[[Elle (magazine)|Elle]] |access-date=3 February 2020}}</ref> Later in the press room, the young Sama Al-Kateab, who had been held by her parents on stage while accepting [[BAFTA Award for Best Documentary|Best Documentary]], was allowed to roam and took to running the length of the stage and playing with microphones.<ref name="10 things" /> With his fifth win for [[BAFTA Award for Best Cinematography|Best Cinematography]] for ''[[1917 (2019 film)|1917]]'', [[Roger Deakins]] becomes the most-decorated BAFTA winner in the category. Collecting his BAFTA, he said: "I think it was [[George Orwell]] that said all films are special but some films are more special than others, and for me this was a really special film." The recent [[Brexit]] was also mentioned, appearing in [[Brad Pitt]]'s acceptance speech for [[BAFTA Award for Best Actor in a Supporting Role|Best Actor in a Supporting Role]] delivered by co-star [[Margot Robbie]]; Pitt's speech also noted that he would name his award "Harry" because he's going to take it to the United States.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.vanityfair.com/hollywood/2020/02/brad-pitt-baftas-prince-william-meghan-markle-joke |title=Brad Pitt's "Megxit" Joke Got an Awkward Laugh From Prince William at the BAFTAs |last=Arsenault |first=Bridget |date=3 February 2020 |work=[[Vanity Fair (magazine)|Vanity Fair]] |access-date=3 February 2020}}</ref>
